nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving lowland rivers, streams, cenotes, lagoons, and spring-fed pools with sand, mud, or limestone substrates and dense shoreline vegetation or woody debris.

decorPreferences: caves or clay pots for spawning
hardy or potted plants (Java fern, Anubias)

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied diet of quality cichlid pellets, flakes, and frozen or live foods (bloodworms, brine shrimp, blackworms). In the wild they tend to feed on small crustaceans, invertebrates, molluscs, and organic detritus sifted from the substrate.

imageCandidates.notes: George Chernilevsky, 3500x2700. Tack-sharp full lateral profile of a live adult showing the diagnostic red-orange throat/belly, black lateral spot, and iridescent blue-speckled fins; clean, well-lit classic hobby presentation.
Bloopityboop, 2880x1616. Sharp in-tank shot of a vividly coloured individual with an intense fiery-red throat and red-pink fins; different angle for variety.
H. Zell, 5216x2997. Sharp high-res lateral of a dominant/breeding-coloured fish displaying strong dark vertical bars plus the red throat and blue fin filaments.

summary: The Firemouth Cichlid (Thorichthys meeki) is a Central American cichlid widely considered one of the more beginner-friendly members of the family. Males display a vivid red-orange throat during territorial or courtship encounters. Best suited to a 200 L tank alongside similarly-sized, robust fish.
careNotes: The Firemouth Cichlid is generally regarded as one of the more peaceful Central American cichlids and is often recommended as a first cichlid for hobbyists moving beyond community fish, though pairs become noticeably territorial during spawning and will defend a substantial area around the nest. Males develop extended fin filaments and the distinctive fiery-red throat — which they inflate into a gular pouch (a fleshy sac under the jaw) to bluff rivals without direct fighting — and can be told apart from females once they reach adult size, though juveniles are difficult to sex. Sand or fine-gravel substrate is preferred because the species sifts mouthfuls of substrate while foraging for food.
breedingNotes: Monogamous biparental substrate spawner. Pairs clean a flat rock, slate, or pit and deposit 100–500 eggs, with both parents guarding eggs and free-swimming fry. Breeding is generally considered straightforward in captivity, but the pair will become aggressively territorial during the cycle and may need to be isolated from smaller or slower tankmates.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Swapped in as primary because the assigned primary (Aquarium Co-Op) has no species page or care guide for Thorichthys meeki — only a user forum breeding journal. SeriouslyFish provided sizing, water parameters, habitat, taxonomy, and behaviour.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (17 cm TL), temperature 26–30 °C, pH 6.5–7.5, dH 0–10, distribution, family/order, and aquarium-trade tank recommendation (~100 cm).

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~15 cm), pH 6.5–8.0, hardness 8–15 dGH, 75–86 °F, lifespan 8–10 years, omnivore diet, semi-territorial temperament, and 30 gal minimum for a pair.

additional.name: Aquarium Tidings
additional.notes: Third hobbyist cross-check: 15 cm adult size, 29 gal minimum / 55 gal recommended, 20–32 °C, pH 6.5–8.5, lifespan ~8 years (up to 15 reported), easy breeding.

fish.breedingNotes: The Firemouth Cichlid is an open substrate spawner that deposits 100–500 eggs on a cleaned flat rock or pit, with both parents sharing guard duties over the eggs and free-swimming fry. Pairs tend to form readily when a small group of juveniles is raised together and allowed to pair up naturally.
